author: Byron
msg:
I am sick of this lack of meaningful communication and "play with words" game. The team obviously think we are mindless plebs that they can play with.

What we all have to understand is that the power is in our hands. This thing is nothing without us, not only because we paid, but also because we offer the decentralization.

We should dictate the rules and priorities. NOT the team. We paid, our money they are using, and we provide the decentralization. Without us this thing is DEAD.

So, for me.

- The team's first priority is COMMUNICATION with US
- The team's second priority is to implement tasks and evolve the product

I am willing to remove all my licenses from the marketplace and stop all promotional/marketing actions until the team behaves and starts treating us like partners. I urge everyone in here, who has invested their hard earned money for just being played (remember the voting fiasco right?), to do the same.

We have the power to change this. Shut down your farms, big or small. Stop promoting. Lets see how long they will keep ignoring us.

Think about it.

author: Don Standard
msg:
Building on the discussion above, here’s how I’m currently thinking about it. 

Setting an artificially low ULO profit share on the marketplace won’t prevent undercutting. It will simply push competition off-platform into Telegram groups and DMs. 

That still results in marketeers losing ULOs they onboarded, while new users are flooded with offers and exposed to bad actors. It also creates a real risk of scams, which reflects badly on Unity as a platform. 

Another unintended consequence is that, if organic leasing becomes harder for some UNOs, it may incentivise consolidation of devices rather than growth of a distributed operator base — which runs counter to the goal of a broad, decentralised edge network. 

A cleaner solution would be for Unity to apply a consistent, platform-defined licence split wherever licences are offered. If no one can offer preferential terms elsewhere, there’s no incentive for DM outreach, licence switching, or spam.
People who put real work into marketing Unity would still have a fair advantage by being able to distribute their own lease codes. Those who don’t wish to, or are unable to market, would still have their licences fairly allocated to new ULOs who join without a code. 

This isn’t about restricting competition — it’s about clear, transparent rules applied equally, protecting users, and keeping activity on the platform rather than in private messages.

author: Byron
msg:
Thank you for your comment. I totally get your point of view and your arguments. Let me clarify a couple of things.

1. I was among the first, probably THE first, to say that the whole voting thing was useless. I thoroughly explained my reasoning and suggested an automated system which would guarantee fair incentives (shares) for both UNOs and ULOs and at the same time allow us to boost marketplace performance and product awareness UNITED, since there would be NO need for individual offers. The optimal share (in terms of revenue and adoption) at any given time would be the one already set by the algorithm, so any deviation from that would directly mean less performance, something that no sensible UNO would want. The marketplace can move to a fully unregulated status when it reaches a certain capacity/maturity in terms of supply (tasks) / demand (quality ULOs).

2. Given the decision from the team for a vote, and a hard fixed UNO/ULO share I had to propose what I believe best fits our tasks as marketeers. What would give us the most tools to promote our licenses LONG TERM, not only when incentives are miniscule as right now. I am sorry if I had you divided. That was not my intention. My only intention is to help in making this project, my investment, successful and rewarding, as soon as possible.

author: Akira
msg:
**TL;DR – Unity License Distribution Proposal**

The current license marketplace forces UNOs to compete against each other for ULOs via reward splits, fragmenting marketing and hurting long-term growth.

This proposal removes the marketplace entirely. Licenses are automatically assigned to ULOs when the Unity app is installed and a license is activated, using a neutral allocation method.

To keep strong marketing incentives, UNOs are rewarded through a referral system: if a user installs the app via a UNO referral and keeps a license active for a minimum period, the referring UNO earns a reward. No instant payouts, no split bidding.

➡️** Result**: less internal competition, aligned incentives, simpler onboarding, and rewards tied to real, long-term adoption rather than short-term tactics.

If this sounds good to any of you and want to see the full proposal in detail, DM me 🤝

author: CloverNodes
msg:
Your proposal is great and I have to give you credit for taking the time, it solves a lot of issues but it introduces some fundamental changes that need to be addressed:

**1) Automatic assignment breaks the incentive loop:** The proposal removes the marketplace and does automatic assignment on install (round-robin / load-balanced / performance weighted)
 The issue is simple, if I bring a user to Unity and they get assigned to someone else why would I market? The proposal tries to solve this with referrals later but that still creates a “marketing dead zone” in the meantime.

**2) It removes ULO choice and community trust:** This proposal makes onboarding “simpler” because users no longer choose operators. But many ULOs want to pick an operator because:
A) They trust them
B) They want support
C) They’re part of that operator’s community

Random assignment makes UNOs interchangeable. That kills relationships, loyalty and the most important part it kills retention.

**3) “Protocol-defined” becomes political fast:** Akira says it would be neutral and protocol-defined. But once you introduce:
- performance weighted assignment
-uptime scoring
-quality metrics
you’ve created the next battleground. Now everyone fights over the algorithm instead of the marketplace.

**4) Referral systems create a new type of abuse:** He does include safeguards like:
-time-locked rewards
-one-time attribution
-caps / decay
-device fingerprinting + wallet -uniqueness + rate limits

That’s good, but in practice referral systems attract:
-fake installs
-churn loops
-phone farms
-referral laundering
So you may remove split wars, but you introduce referral warfare.

**5) The timing is backwards:** In the doc, referrals really “pay” when the network reaches capacity and licenses are scarce, like via airdrops. But incentives are needed most RIGHT NOW, while adoption is still being built. You don’t remove the main incentive today and promise rewards later.
Akira’s proposal is thoughtful and I agree with the problem it’s trying to solve, race-to-the-bottom splits are not healthy. But removing the marketplace entirely breaks the incentive loop for UNOs to market, it also removes ULO choice and it shifts conflict from splits to an algorithm.

**The better fix is simple:** keep the marketplace, set a minimum standard split, keep private deals free market and add referrals as a bonus layer on the ULO side to drive adoption.  That keeps builders incentivized today while still eliminating the worst marketplace behavior.

author: CloverNodes
msg:
Referral systems sound great on paper but they always attract people trying to game it. Fake installs are when someone downloads the app just to trigger a referral reward then never use it.

Churn loops happen when people activate for the minimum time.. cash out whatever reward then drop off and repeat.

Phone farms someone runs 20 to 200 phones to simulate “new users” and farms referrals which is hard to combat 

Referral laundering are when groups trade referral codes and recycle signups to make it look like real growth, even though it’s just the same circle of people. So you might remove split wars in the marketplace but you replace it with a different battle who’s is people competing to exploit referrals instead of competing to provide better value.

The proposal does mention time-locks, one-time attribution and Sybil resistance which helps but it’s still a real risk in any referral-based system which in my opinion needs to be thought of very well before being introduced

author: CloverNodes
msg:
Love this reply and I’m glad you clarified it because that addresses my biggest concern which is the “incentive loop” for UNOs marketing. I actually agree that both can co-exist… short term incentives + long term incentives makes sense.

My only pushback is this:

If we remove the marketplace and rely primarily on future airdrops as the long term incentive it can become too delayed and too uncertain for most UNOs to justify real marketing spend today. Most builders won’t grind 60 to 90 days or longer on a “maybe later” reward even if the vision is solid. Also referral reward in the form of future licenses is a good idea but it still introduces questions like:
how often airdrops happen?
how the weight is calculated?
How quickly UNOs feel the payoff?
what happens if the network pace slows?

So I’m aligned with your direction but I think the safest version is this:

Keep the marketplace with a minimum standard split preferably weighted towards an UNO initially so 60/40 split (so we stop the race to the bottom) keep private leasing fully flexible and add referral rewards on top on the ULO side (both % revenue and future license weighting) That way we don’t break anything that’s working today but we still evolve into the more cooperative model long term.

Really appreciate you pushing this conversation forward I gotta run but I’ll reply back on any counter points later on just tag me.
